quiz.title: Knowledge Check
quiz.summary: Test what you picked up about Afrobeat and Amapiano with a guided quiz flow that now includes real listening challenges.

intro.description: Move through a mix of concept checks and real listening clips, get immediate feedback, and see how well you can separate the two genres by ear.
highlights: Instant answer feedback after every choice
Final score with result tier and answer review

resultBands.title: Genre guide
resultBands.message: You can clearly distinguish Afrobeat and Amapiano across sound, origin, and performance feel.

resultBands.title: On the right track
resultBands.message: You understand the main differences, but a quick review will help lock in the details.

resultBands.title: Keep exploring
resultBands.message: You have the big picture started. Revisit the learning path and try again with fresh ears.

questions.prompt: Which genre started in South Africa and is closely tied to piano-led grooves and log drum bass lines?
questions.context: Think about each genre's musical roots.
questions.correctFeedback: Correct. Amapiano emerged from South African house and jazz influences and is known for piano phrases and log drum rhythms.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Amapiano is the South African genre recognized for piano textures and the deep log drum pulse.
questions.insight: Afrobeat is rooted in West Africa, especially Nigeria, while Amapiano developed in South African townships.

questions.prompt: You hear a song driven by roomy percussion, live-band energy, and a politically charged performance style. Which genre does that most strongly suggest?
questions.context: Focus on the overall band feel rather than one instrument.
questions.correctFeedback: Correct. Afrobeat is strongly associated with live instrumentation, layered percussion, and performance rooted in message and movement.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. That kind of live-band, groove-heavy energy points more toward Afrobeat than Amapiano.
questions.insight: Classic Afrobeat often blends horns, guitars, percussion, and socially conscious themes into long-form grooves.

questions.prompt: Which description best matches a common Amapiano listening experience?
questions.context: Think about pacing and dancefloor atmosphere.
options: Fast distorted drums with punk guitars
Slow-burn house groove with airy keys and deep bass
Acoustic ballad with string quartet backing
Marching-band rhythm with brass stabs only
questions.correctFeedback: Correct. Amapiano often feels spacious and hypnotic, built on soft keys, rolling percussion, and deep bass motion.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Amapiano is usually recognized by its slower, atmospheric house groove with airy piano and bass movement.
questions.insight: Its dance energy is often subtle and immersive rather than aggressive or rushed.

questions.prompt: Which artist or scene would more likely be discussed when tracing the rise of Afrobeat?
questions.context: Choose the answer most connected to the genre's historical identity.
options: Fela Kuti and Lagos live-band culture
questions.correctFeedback: Correct. Fela Kuti is central to Afrobeat's history and its Lagos live-band movement.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Afrobeat history is strongly linked to Fela Kuti and the Lagos performance scene.
questions.insight: Amapiano has its own important scenes and artists, but Afrobeat's foundation is typically introduced through Fela Kuti's influence.

questions.prompt: If a listener says, "This track feels like a laid-back groove built for smooth dancing and repeated piano motifs," which genre are they probably describing?
questions.context: Apply the genre traits instead of recalling a fact word-for-word.
questions.correctFeedback: Correct. The laid-back dance groove and repeated piano phrasing are classic clues for Amapiano.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. That smooth, piano-driven dance feel is usually associated with Amapiano.
questions.insight: Afrobeat can be danceable too, but its identity is less centered on those floating piano motifs.

questions.prompt: Listen to the clip and choose the genre that fits it best.
questions.context: Let the groove settle first, then decide whether it feels more live-band Afrobeat or smoother Amapiano.

audio.label: Listening clip 1
audio.caption: Focus on the percussion energy, the vocal presence, and whether the groove feels band-led or club-led.

questions.correctFeedback: Correct. The clip leans Afrobeat with its lively vocal presence, grounded groove, and more performance-driven rhythmic energy.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. This clip fits Afrobeat more than Amapiano because it feels more vocal-forward and live-groove driven.
questions.insight: This listening clip is "Woman" by Rema. The clue is less about memorizing the song and more about noticing the rhythmic lift and performance energy.

questions.prompt: Listen to the clip and choose the genre that fits it best.
questions.context: Pay attention to the pulse underneath the groove and whether the track feels airy and hypnotic or more stage-driven.

audio.label: Listening clip 2
audio.caption: Listen for floating piano texture, smoother bounce, and a deep bass pattern underneath the vocals.

questions.correctFeedback: Correct. The clip fits Amapiano with its lighter, smoother feel and deep bass-led dance groove.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. This one is Amapiano because the rhythm feels more hypnotic and club-oriented than Afrobeat.
questions.insight: This listening clip is "Water" by Tyla. The big clue is the floating texture and log-drum-style pulse rather than a live-band push.

questions.prompt: Listen to the clip and choose the genre that fits it best.
questions.context: Use what you learned about tempo feel and instrumentation instead of focusing on the artist name.

audio.label: Listening clip 3
audio.caption: Notice whether the rhythm feels grounded and percussive or more spacious and piano-led.
listenFor: Less of the airy piano wash heard in Amapiano

questions.correctFeedback: Correct. This clip reads as Afrobeat because the groove lands with a more direct percussive drive and less of an Amapiano float.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. The rhythmic shape here is closer to Afrobeat than Amapiano.
questions.insight: This listening clip is "Kese" by Wizkid. The cue is the grounded pulse and percussive movement rather than a deep rolling log-drum bounce.

questions.prompt: Listen to the clip and choose the genre that fits it best.
questions.context: Ask yourself whether the beat feels club-hypnotic and bass-led or more like a live-band groove.

audio.label: Listening clip 4
audio.caption: Listen for a repetitive dance pulse, a smoother bounce, and a deep bass personality.

questions.correctFeedback: Correct. The clip fits Amapiano with its repetitive dance pulse and deeper bass-led momentum.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. This one lands in Amapiano because of its hypnotic bounce and club-oriented low-end.
questions.insight: This listening clip is "Tshwala Bam". The strongest clue is the rolling, bass-centered groove rather than live-band percussion.

questions.prompt: Listen to the clip and choose the genre that fits it best.
questions.context: This last listening question is about confidence: pick the genre whose overall feel matches the clip best.

audio.label: Listening clip 5
audio.caption: Do not chase every detail. Decide whether the whole track leans more toward Afrobeat's performance feel or Amapiano's club glide.

questions.correctFeedback: Correct. The clip leans Afrobeat because it feels more vocal-driven and rhythmically assertive than Amapiano.
questions.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. This clip fits Afrobeat more than Amapiano because the overall energy is more direct and performance-centered.
questions.insight: This listening clip is "Holy Ghost" by Omah Lay. By this point, the goal is to recognize the bigger feel of the groove, not just one instrument.
